trajectory_summary: The call opens with a cooperative customer and collapses within 90 seconds because the rep substituted a Google Maps site assessment for the verify conversation, then issued two successive unilateral disqualifications the design team never authorized. John pushed back clearly and the rep's response was to add a second exit reason rather than route him. The call ends with a vague callback promise — no appointment, no clean DQ, no data collected.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — the correct classification is 'lost lead / premature exit.' This is not a DQ: the rep made a site-assessment judgment that belongs to the design team, John contested it with relevant information (partial-day shading, one side of the roof), and the rep did not route him. A qualified lead may have been abandoned.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Setter scope discipline — design-team work belongs to the design team
primary_coaching_focus.quote: So I see that you have some trees around you. Are all of those trees still there?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Every failure on this call traces back to the rep conducting a satellite-image site assessment mid-call instead of completing the verify section. Holland needs to be able to name the boundary: shading analysis, roof-space evaluation, and solar viability judgments are design-team scope. The setter's job is to qualify and schedule. Once Holland owns that boundary, the DQ at 01:24, the second disqualifier at 02:20, and the abandoned verify section all resolve as the same error.

key_moments.quote: We've only been renting it for about a little over a year, but, we've owned the home for a long time.
key_moments.stewart_read: John's phrasing is genuinely ambiguous. 'Renting it for about a little over a year' could mean (a) he is a landlord who recently moved back in, (b) he is a tenant who does not own the property, or (c) he is describing a prior rental period before purchasing. The rep responds 'Okay. Very nice.' and moves on without probing. Per dq_rules.not_homeowner, renters are a hard DQ — the rep needed one clarifying question here before proceeding.

key_moments.quote: How long have you been living in this home for?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the second and last verify item the rep completes. The call ends with zero data collected on interest_in_solar, utility_company, average_bill, roof_type_age, prior_designs, or title/co-owners. The rep abandoned the verify section after two items to conduct a Google Maps site assessment. A complete verify failure means the rep has no qualification data to support a callback, no bill anchor, and no emotional hook — even if the design team clears the shading question, there is nothing to work with.

key_moments.quote: So I see that you have some trees around you. Are all of those trees still there?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the origin point of the call's failure. The rep is on Google Maps mid-call and pivots the conversation to a satellite-image shading assessment before completing the verify section. This is setter scope creep in its clearest form: the rep is doing design-team work — site evaluation — rather than qualification work. The setter's job is to gather information and schedule the closer; shading analysis from a satellite image is not in that scope.

key_moments.quote: So I think that with those trees still there, I don't think that the panels will be able to get enough sun, for them to be super fully fully beneficial to you.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep issues a unilateral shading disqualification based on a Google Maps satellite image, without design-team input, without completing verify, and before John has raised any concern. This is not a DQ the setter is authorized to make — shading analysis requires the design team. The rep's own language at 02:25 ('I can reach out to my design team') confirms the design team is the correct authority, which makes this DQ retroactively self-contradicting.
